records.notes: Cold-weather gilled mushroom on wood.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Wild velvet foot is edible when confidently identified and cooked, but it is a winter gilled mushroom with deadly look-alikes. Keep wild brown-stemmed forms separate from cultivated enoki in your head.
records.edibility_detail: Confirm clustered growth on wood, orange-brown sticky caps, white spore print, no ring, and a dark velvety stem base. Do not eat any Galerina-like brown gilled mushroom.
records.general_notes: The dark velvety stem base matters here. This is one of the few edible-type gilled mushrooms worth checking in cold weather.

mushroom_profile.season_note: October, November, December, January, February, March.

records.field_identification: Small to medium orange-brown caps in clusters on dead hardwood, often in cold weather. Gills are pale, spore print white, there is no ring, and the lower stem becomes dark and velvety.
records.mushroom_card_note: Winter edible only with careful ID; deadly Galerina is the ugly little goblin in this room.
records.caution_reason: White spore print, no ring, and a dark velvety stem base are required; deadly galerina grows on wood too and has rusty-brown spores.
records.look_alike_notes: Do not eat wild enoki without confirming a white spore print and a dark velvety ringless stem; deadly galerina grows on wood too and has rusty-brown spores.

records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelle-type mushrooms when positively identified. Cook; do not confuse with jack-o'-lanterns or true-gilled orange mushrooms.
records.field_identification: Ground-growing chanterelle relatives with blunt false gills, ridges, wrinkles, or a smooth underside rather than thin blade-like true gills. The false gills usually run down the stem, fork, cross-vein, or look melted into the cap. Flesh is usually firm and pale inside, often with a fruity or apricot-like smell. Do not identify by orange/yellow color alone.
records.mushroom_card_note: Conifer-associated yellow chanterelle complex; confirm blunt false gills, not true blade-like gills.

taste: mild to slightly peppery in some species; not a primary beginner ID trait

records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when confidently identified by false gills/ridges, ground growth, firm flesh, and appropriate habitat. Do not eat orange mushrooms with true blade-like gills or mushrooms growing in dense clusters from wood or buried roots.

records.look_alike_notes: Main concern is jack-o’-lantern mushrooms. Jack-o’-lanterns have true blade-like gills, often grow in dense clusters from wood, stumps, buried roots, or tree bases, and are typically deeper pumpkin-orange. Chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt ridges or false gills rather than separable true gills. False chanterelles and other orange gilled mushrooms should also be separated by true gills, softer texture, and different growth habits.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject slimy, brown-edged, waterlogged, moldy, or bug-heavy specimens. Reject specimens where the underside has sharp, separable true gills. Reject orange clustered mushrooms from wood, stumps, or buried roots.
records.image_needs: Needs underside macro showing false gills/ridges, side view showing ridges running down stem, split specimen showing pale/solid flesh, habitat shot showing ground growth, and chanterelle vs jack-o’-lantern comparison.

records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelle-type mushrooms when positively identified. Cook; check false gills, growth from soil, and lack of jack-o'-lantern clustering on wood.
records.field_identification: Ground-growing chanterelle relatives with blunt false gills, ridges, wrinkles, or a smooth underside rather than thin blade-like true gills. The false gills usually run down the stem, fork, cross-vein, or look melted into the cap. Flesh is usually firm and pale inside, often with a fruity or apricot-like smell. Do not identify by orange/yellow color alone.
records.mushroom_card_note: Hardwood yellow chanterelle complex; false gills/ridges are the key separator from jack-o'-lantern.

taste: mild to slightly peppery in some species; not a primary beginner ID trait

records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when confidently identified by false gills/ridges, ground growth, firm flesh, and appropriate habitat. Do not eat orange mushrooms with true blade-like gills or mushrooms growing in dense clusters from wood or buried roots.

Chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt ridges or false gills rather than separable true gills. False chanterelles and other orange gilled mushrooms should also be separated by true gills, softer texture, and different growth habits.

records.notes: Yellow fly agaric entry.
records.edibility_detail: Do not eat. Contains psychoactive/toxic compounds and can cause serious illness, confusion, vomiting, and unsafe intoxication.
records.general_notes: A familiar northern Amanita and a useful safety record because it teaches cap warts, ring, white gills, and bulbous base checks.

edible_use.notes: Do not eat. Contains psychoactive/toxic compounds and can cause serious illness, confusion, vomiting, and unsafe intoxication.

records.scientific_name: Amanita muscaria var. guessowii / Amanita chrysoblema yellow-orange form
records.field_identification: Bright yellow to orange Amanita with white warts on the cap, white gills, ring on the stem, and a bulbous base with veil remnants. Often with birch, pine, spruce, or mixed woods in northern areas.
records.mushroom_card_note: Yellow/orange fly agaric: white warts, white gills, ring, bulbous base. Do not eat.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Amanita overview
use_links.notes: Amanita structures: volva, ring, white gills, universal veil remnants.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Detailed culinary and caution notes are still being copied back.
records.general_notes: Representative Commons images currently use Morchella esculenta / yellow-morel-type material for this field-guide group record.
records.look_alike_notes: Every morel must be cut lengthwise before eating; discard any specimen that is not hollow from cap tip to stem base or has a wrinkled/brain-like cap instead of true pits. Link false morels/Gyromitra and Verpa-style early false morels as primary dangers.

records.culinary_uses: Good fresh or dried; small mushrooms dry well and rehydrate into soups and sauces.
records.general_notes: Useful late-season chanterelle relative. The hollow yellow foot and blunt ridges are better clues than cap color.
records.field_identification: Ground-growing chanterelle relatives with blunt false gills, ridges, wrinkles, or a smooth underside rather than thin blade-like true gills. The false gills usually run down the stem, fork, cross-vein, or look melted into the cap. Flesh is usually firm and pale inside, often with a fruity or apricot-like smell. Do not identify by orange/yellow color alone.
records.mushroom_card_note: Small late-season chanterelle relative: hollow yellow foot and blunt decurrent ridges.

taste: mild to slightly peppery in some species; not a primary beginner ID trait

records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when confidently identified by false gills/ridges, ground growth, firm flesh, and appropriate habitat. Do not eat orange mushrooms with true blade-like gills or mushrooms growing in dense clusters from wood or buried roots.

edible_use.notes: Good edible after confirming blunt decurrent ridges, hollow yellowish stem, and soil/moss habitat. Avoid small brown gilled mushrooms with true gills.

Jack-o’-lanterns have true blade-like gills, often grow in dense clusters from wood, stumps, buried roots, or tree bases, and are typically deeper pumpkin-orange. Chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt ridges or false gills rather than separable true gills. False chanterelles and other orange gilled mushrooms should also be separated by true gills, softer texture, and different growth habits.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject slimy, brown-edged, waterlogged, moldy, or bug-heavy specimens. Reject specimens where the underside has sharp, separable true gills. Reject orange clustered mushrooms from wood, stumps, or buried roots.

records.edibility_detail: Generally treated as edible when confirmed, but identification must rule out unsafe gilled mushrooms and contaminated urban mulch sites.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when confidently identified and cooked. Avoid old, bug-filled, or polluted-site specimens; do not treat any lawn/mulch mushroom as food without spore print and ring/gill confirmation.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this guide entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this guide entry yet.
records.overview: Large woodchip-loving edible gilled mushroom often cultivated in mulch beds; useful for the app because it is a major missing gilled edible.
records.notes: Large woodchip-loving edible gilled mushroom often cultivated in mulch beds; useful for the app because it is a major missing gilled edible.
records.general_notes: Large woodchip-loving edible gilled mushroom often cultivated in mulch beds; useful for the app because it is a major missing gilled edible.
records.field_identification: Wine-red to reddish-brown caps fading tan; purple-gray gills darkening with spores; thick cogwheel-like ring; white stem; often in woodchips or rich mulched ground. Dark purple-brown to purplish-black spore print.
records.commonness: Occasional to common in mulched habitats

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Stropharia rugosoannulata
use_links.notes: Wine-red caps fading brown, purple-gray gills/spore print, cogwheel ring, woodchip habitat.

use_links.label: MAMI: Stropharia rugoso-annulata
use_links.notes: Midwestern certification-study entry; purple-black spore print and gill color progression.

records.edibility_detail: Edible when confidently identified and cooked, but this entry is a group-level field record because North American wood-ear names have shifted. Host wood and overall jelly-ear form matter more here than old European names in field guides.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when confidently identified and cooked; valued more for chewy texture than flavor. Use fresh, clean specimens from sound wood, or dry and rehydrate them for soups, stir-fries, and brothy dishes. Avoid old, blackened, polluted, or slimy-decomposing specimens.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this guide entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this guide entry yet.
records.overview: Wood ears are brown, rubbery-gelatinous fungi that form ear-shaped, cup-shaped, or irregular floppy fruiting bodies on dead wood after wet weather. In North America, older field guides often used European wood-ear names, but current sources separate similar-looking hardwood and conifer species. For field use in Upper Michigan, this entry treats them as a wood-ear group and leans on substrate: hardwood logs and branches suggest an Auricularia angiospermarum-type wood ear, while conifer wood suggests an Auricularia americana-type wood ear.
records.notes: Brown ear-shaped to cup-shaped jelly fungi on dead wood; substrate matters because North American wood-ear taxonomy separates hardwood and conifer associates more than older field guides did.
records.general_notes: A field-useful wood-ear group entry. Record the host wood when possible: hardwood and conifer collections may represent different North American Auricularia species even when they look nearly identical.
records.field_identification: Look for thin brown to reddish-brown gelatinous ears, cups, or floppy lobes attached directly to dead wood. The outer surface may look finely hairy or frosted when fresh; the fertile surface is smoother and often wrinkled or veined. They dry dark and tough, then revive after rain. Note the host wood carefully, because substrate is one of the best field clues for separating North American wood-ear species.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring through fall after wet weather; dried fruiting bodies can persist and revive after rain.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Auricularia americana
use_links.notes: North American jelly-ear taxonomy, shape, substrate, and similar jelly fungi.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert wood-fungus page: Auricularia angiospermarum
use_links.notes: Hardwood wood-ear page and comparison with conifer-associated A. americana.

records.look_alike_notes: Brown jelly fungi such as Exidia species can look similar, but they usually form more blob-like, brain-like, or cushiony masses rather than thin ear-shaped cups. Leafy Tremella-like jelly fungi can form clustered folds, but they are usually more frilly or fused into masses instead of distinct ear-like lobes. Substrate and shape matter more here than color alone.

records.edibility_detail: Edible but uncommon; this guide should emphasize conservation-minded foraging and strong ID before harvest.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when confidently identified and fresh, but harvest conservatively; if only one fruiting body is found, leave it or document it rather than taking it.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this guide entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this guide entry yet.
records.overview: Important MAMI-listed species and useful look-alike/comparison entry for hen-of-the-woods style clusters, but should be treated conservatively because it is not commonly encountered.
records.notes: Important MAMI-listed species and useful look-alike/comparison entry for hen-of-the-woods style clusters, but should be treated conservatively because it is not commonly encountered.
records.general_notes: Important MAMI-listed species and useful look-alike/comparison entry for hen-of-the-woods style clusters, but should be treated conservatively because it is not commonly encountered.
records.field_identification: Compound cluster with many small round umbrella-like caps branching from a central/base stalk; pores, not gills; fruits on the ground near hardwood roots, stumps, or buried wood. Reported as uncommon/rare in North America.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Polyporus umbellatus
use_links.notes: Fruits terrestrially near hardwood bases/roots; rare in North America.

use_links.label: MAMI: Polyporus umbellatus
use_links.notes: Dimensions and identifying characteristics for umbrella polypore.

records.scientific_name: Lactarius / Lactifluus spp.; includes volemus-type, hygrophoroides-type, indigo, orange-latex, and related milkcap groups
records.edibility_detail: Some milk caps are good edibles, but this group stays caution-level unless the entry is a well-supported species. Confirm latex color, latex color-change, gill spacing, odor, host tree, and staining behavior. Avoid acrid/hot, poorly identified, old, dry, or latex-free specimens unless the species is confirmed by other traits.
records.culinary_uses: Some milk caps are good edibles when confidently identified and cooked, but the group is not beginner-simple. Avoid acrid, peppery, bitter, or poorly identified milk caps.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this guide entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this guide entry yet.
records.overview: Broad edible/caution group for milk caps because Lactarius/Lactifluus are a major missing gilled category and require latex-color filtering.
records.notes: Practical field treatment is safer than pretending every orange-brown, mild milkcap can be cleanly species-keyed in the app. Lactifluus volemus is recognizable when classic, but similar edible/nearby taxa differ in gill spacing, odor, and latex staining.
records.general_notes: Broad edible/caution group for milk caps because Lactarius/Lactifluus are a major missing gilled category and require latex-color filtering.
records.field_identification: Gilled mushrooms that exude latex or “milk” when the gills or flesh are cut. Identification depends heavily on latex color, how quickly the latex changes color, whether it stains the flesh or paper, cap zoning, gill spacing, odor, taste/acridity, and host trees. Do not identify milk caps by cap color alone.

gill_color: Pale cream to yellowish or orange-tinged; attached to decurrent; close in L. volemus, more distant in L. hygrophoroides.
stem_color: Colored like cap or paler; solid to stuffed; no ring or volva.
latex_color_initial: scant or absent in old/dry specimens
latex_color_change: orange with no green staining in Lactarius thyinos
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Latex can be scant in old or dry specimens, so absence of milk is not a confident ID.
staining: Classic L. volemus develops brown staining on cut tissue, hands, paper, and damaged areas. Some similar taxa remain paler or stain less strongly.
host_filter_tokens: hardwoods, especially oaks for L. hygrophoroides
mixed eastern North American hardwood woods
stem_features: same color as cap or paler

records.look_alike_notes: Milk caps are a field-ID system, not a single trait. Latex color and color-change timing are critical. Some species have white latex that turns yellow or brown; some orange-latex species stain green; Lactarius thyinos is notable for orange milk without green bruising. Acrid or hot-tasting milk caps should not be treated as edible beginner species. The true European Lactarius deliciosus name has often been misapplied in North America, so use regional North American names/groups when possible.

edible_use.notes: Edible group for advanced confirmation only; use this entry only when it shows mild taste, white latex behavior, no acrid burn, no yellowing latex, and no ring/volva. Not beginner-safe by color alone.

records.image_needs: Needs fresh cut gills showing latex, same cut after 30 seconds/5 minutes/30–60 minutes, latex staining paper/tissue, cap zoning/scrobicules, gill spacing comparison, and host/habitat images.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Old/dry specimens may not produce latex; do not force an ID from dried-out material. Reject moldy, waterlogged, sour-smelling, insect-heavy, or weakly identified specimens. Avoid acrid/hot milk caps unless the app has a specific, regionally appropriate edible treatment.

records.edibility_detail: Edible with caution; species-level Armillaria ID is tricky and reactions are common enough to emphasize careful cooking and small first portions.
records.culinary_uses: Edible only when confidently identified and cooked; honey mushrooms cause GI upset for some people. Avoid raw, old, or poorly confirmed specimens.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this guide entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this guide entry yet.
records.overview: Bulbous honey mushroom is an Armillaria species usually found on the ground or near hardwood bases, often attached to buried roots. It tends to be smaller and looser than classic honey mushroom clusters, with a swollen/bulbous stem base and a cobwebby or short-lived ring zone.
records.notes: Species-level split for the honey mushroom complex so the app does not treat all honeys as one vague bucket.
records.general_notes: Species-level split for the honey mushroom complex so the app does not treat all honeys as one vague bucket.
records.field_identification: Look for tan to pinkish-brown caps with tiny yellowish-brown scales, whitish gills that run slightly down the stem, white spore print, swollen stem base, yellowish ring zone or flimsy ring, and possible black rhizomorphs near buried wood or roots.
records.foraging_value: Fair to good edible for experienced foragers only

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Armillaria gallica
use_links.notes: Bulbous base, loose growth, late season, white spores, ring zone, and hardwood/root association.

records.look_alike_notes: Honey mushrooms have white spores and Armillaria-style growth on wood, roots, or buried wood. Deadly galerina has rusty-brown spores and small brown caps; sulfur tuft has yellow-green gills and purple-brown spores; brick cap has gray to purple-gray gills and brick-red caps; shaggy scalycap has heavy scales and cinnamon-brown spores; jack-o-lanterns are orange, clustered on wood, and poisonous. Do not eat honey mushrooms unless a mature cap gives a white spore print; deadly galerina can grow on the same wood and has a rusty-brown spore print, while jack-o’-lanterns can share the white spore print but have bright orange flesh and true orange gills.
records.caution_reason: Honey mushroom group with toxic wood-growing look-alikes; white spore print and Armillaria traits are required.
records.flavor_texture_notes: Quality is best in young firm caps; stems can be fibrous.

records.edibility_detail: Edible with caution; ringless honey ID requires white spore print, wood/root growth, clustered habit, and exclusion of poisonous look-alikes.
records.culinary_uses: Edible only when confidently identified and cooked; not a beginner mushroom. Must be separated from jack-o’-lanterns and other clustered gilled mushrooms.
records.medicinal_uses: No reliable medicinal use is recorded for this guide entry unless noted in Other uses or source links.
records.other_uses: No practical non-food use is recorded for this guide entry yet.
records.overview: Ringless honey mushrooms are honey-like clustered wood/root mushrooms without a true ring. In this guide they remain a group-level caution entry because North American ringless honey taxonomy has shifted and because clustered ringless gilled mushrooms are easy to over-trust.
records.notes: Ringless honey group added to separate no-ring honey mushrooms from classic ringed Armillaria and to improve gilled mushroom coverage.
records.general_notes: Ringless honey group added to separate no-ring honey mushrooms from classic ringed Armillaria and to improve gilled mushroom coverage.
records.field_identification: Look for dense clusters from wood, stumps, tree bases, or buried roots; tan to honey-brown caps; pale attached to slightly decurrent gills; white spore print; and no true ring on the stem. The missing ring does not make the mushroom automatically safe.
records.commonness: Needs regional review; more southern/warm-region tendency
records.foraging_value: Fair edible for experienced foragers only

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Armillaria tabescens
use_links.notes: Ringless honey mushroom: clusters on hardwoods, no ring/ring zone, eastern North America from Great Lakes southward.

use_links.label: Missouri Dept. of Conservation: Ringless honey mushroom
use_links.notes: Field traits: honey-colored, dry/scaly cap, no ring, clusters at tree bases/stumps, white spore print.

records.look_alike_notes: Honey mushrooms have white spores and Armillaria-style growth on wood, roots, or buried wood. Deadly galerina has rusty-brown spores and small brown caps; sulfur tuft has yellow-green gills and purple-brown spores; brick cap has gray to purple-gray gills and brick-red caps; shaggy scalycap has heavy scales and cinnamon-brown spores; jack-o-lanterns are orange, clustered on wood, and poisonous. Do not eat honey mushrooms unless a mature cap gives a white spore print; deadly galerina can grow on the same wood and has a rusty-brown spore print, while jack-o’-lanterns can share the white spore print but have bright orange flesh and true orange gills.
records.caution_reason: Ringless honeys lack the ring beginners often expect; separate from jack-o-lantern, sulfur tuft, and deadly galerina with spore print, color, gill, and substrate clues.
records.flavor_texture_notes: Use only young firm specimens; older clusters become tough or buggy.

records.overview: Fall wood-clustering Hypholoma with a brick-red cap center, paler margin, gray to purple-gray gills, and purple-brown spore print. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution species and as a comparison point for sulfur tuft and honey mushrooms.
records.notes: Fall wood-clustering Hypholoma with a brick-red cap center, paler margin, gray to purple-gray gills, and purple-brown spore print. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution species and as a comparison point for sulfur tuft and honey mushrooms.
records.general_notes: Fall wood-clustering Hypholoma with a brick-red cap center, paler margin, gray to purple-gray gills, and purple-brown spore print. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution species and as a comparison point for sulfur tuft and honey mushrooms.
records.field_identification: Look for tight clusters on decaying hardwood logs and stumps; brick-red cap center with paler pinkish-buff margin; attached gray to purple-gray gills; yellowish-white stem that may bruise or stain yellow; purple-brown spore print.
records.culinary_uses: Some experienced foragers eat young brick caps, but this guide treats them as advanced/caution because they overlap with other wood-clustering Hypholoma and honey-mushroom look-alikes. Do not eat without expert-level confirmation.
records.edibility_detail: Advanced edible-with-caution record, not a beginner mushroom. Separate carefully from sulfur tuft and other clustered wood mushrooms.

edible_use.notes: Advanced edible-with-caution record, not a beginner mushroom. Separate carefully from sulfur tuft and other clustered wood mushrooms.

records.look_alike_notes: Sulfur tuft is usually more yellow-green, bitter, and often on conifer deadwood; brick cap has a brick-red center, paler margin, gray to purple-gray gills, and favors hardwood stumps/logs. Honey mushrooms have white spores and Armillaria-style rings/rhizomorphs rather than purple-brown spores.

staining: Stem may bruise or stain yellow
mushroom_profile.season_note: Fall on hardwood stumps and logs.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Hypholoma sublateritium
use_links.notes: Brick-red cap, hardwood stump/log clusters, purple-gray gills, and purple-brown spore print.

records.mushroom_card_note: Brick-red clustered hardwood mushroom; advanced ID only because sulfur tuft and honey mushrooms can confuse the picture.

records.overview: Brown-capped wood mushroom with free gills that start pale and become pink as spores mature. Included as a common gilled wood mushroom and pink-spored teaching entry.
records.notes: Brown-capped wood mushroom with free gills that start pale and become pink as spores mature. Included as a common gilled wood mushroom and pink-spored teaching entry.
records.general_notes: Brown-capped wood mushroom with free gills that start pale and become pink as spores mature. Included as a common gilled wood mushroom and pink-spored teaching entry.
records.field_identification: Look for a brown to gray-brown cap on dead wood or buried wood, free gills that do not attach to the stem, gills turning pink with age, brownish-pink spore print, no ring, no volva, and a whitish stem often marked with brownish fibrils.
records.culinary_uses: Edible in some field guides but not a beginner food here; quality is variable and the Pluteus cervinus concept includes cryptic relatives. Use as an ID-learning species unless expert-confirmed.
records.edibility_detail: Edible-with-caution / not a beginner mushroom. The pink spore print and free gills are useful ID clues, but species limits can be messy.

edible_use.notes: Edible-with-caution / not a beginner mushroom. The pink spore print and free gills are useful ID clues, but species limits can be messy.

records.look_alike_notes: The free gills that turn pink and the brownish-pink spore print separate deer mushroom from white-spored Amanitas. Always check for a volva and ring on any pale-gilled mushroom; deadly Amanitas have white spores and a volva/ring pattern, not pink Pluteus spores.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Pluteus cervinus
use_links.notes: Free gills becoming pink, brown cap, wood substrate, and Pluteus cervinus group caveats.

records.mushroom_card_note: Brown wood mushroom with free gills that turn pink; useful for learning pink-spored gilled mushrooms.

records.overview: Small tan grassland mushroom famous for arcs or rings, but many unrelated mushrooms also form fairy rings. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution and cautionary lawn-mushroom entry.
records.notes: Small tan grassland mushroom famous for arcs or rings, but many unrelated mushrooms also form fairy rings. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution and cautionary lawn-mushroom entry.
records.general_notes: Small tan grassland mushroom famous for arcs or rings, but many unrelated mushrooms also form fairy rings. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution and cautionary lawn-mushroom entry.
records.field_identification: Look for small pale tan to buff caps in grass, well-spaced white to buff gills that attach to the stem but do not run down it, a white spore print, and a tough pliant stem that bends rather than snapping easily. Fairy-ring growth alone is not enough for identification.
records.culinary_uses: Edible in many guides, but this guide treats it as advanced because lawn mushrooms include dangerous look-alikes. Do not eat fairy-ring mushrooms without confirming every trait, especially white spore print, well-spaced gills, tough stem, and grassy habitat.
records.edibility_detail: Advanced edible-with-caution. The common name causes confusion because many mushrooms grow in rings.

edible_use.notes: Advanced edible-with-caution. The common name causes confusion because many mushrooms grow in rings.

records.look_alike_notes: Do not use the ring pattern alone: green-spored parasol, meadow mushrooms, and poisonous pale funnels can also appear in grass or rings. Fairy ring mushroom is smaller, tan, white-spored, has well-spaced gills, and a tough pliant stem; fool’s funnel has more crowded decurrent gills and muscarine risk.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Marasmius oreades
use_links.notes: Grass habitat, well-spaced pale gills, white spore print, pale tan cap, and tough pliant stem.

records.mushroom_card_note: Small tan grass mushroom; fairy rings alone do not identify it.

records.notes: This is one of the few milkcaps where a species-level entry is practical: blue fruit body plus blue latex are field-distinctive, with only a few partial look-alikes.
records.field_identification: Latex: indigo blue to dark blue initially; blue latex and damaged tissue slowly stain greenish. Blue to gray-blue, often zoned; convex becoming depressed or funnel-shaped; can be sticky when young. Blue gills, attached to slightly decurrent, close; stain greenish when damaged or aging. Blue to gray-blue, central or slightly off-center; solid when young, sometimes hollowing with age; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Blue to gray-blue, often zoned; convex becoming depressed or funnel-shaped; can be sticky when young.
gill_color: Blue gills, attached to slightly decurrent, close; stain greenish when damaged or aging.
stem_color: Blue to gray-blue, central or slightly off-center; solid when young, sometimes hollowing with age; no ring or volva.
mushroom_profile.latex_abundance: variable; older/dry specimens may produce little latex
mushroom_profile.latex_color_change: blue latex and damaged tissue slowly stain greenish
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Blue latex is the central field character.
staining: Fresh blue surfaces fade gray-blue with age; cut or bruised tissue can shift greenish.
taste: Taste may be mild to slightly acrid. Do not promote acrid specimens as beginner food.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Lactarius paradoxus: can be blue-gray when young but has reddish-brown to purple-brown latex/gills rather than true blue latex do not treat as L. indigo without blue latex Lactarius chelidonium: yellowish/dingy cap tones and yellowish to brown latex, not indigo-blue milk separate; avoid uncertain blue-gray milkcaps
records.edibility_detail: Generally treated as edible, though texture/flavor quality varies. App posture should be: edible only with unmistakable blue latex and blue/green-staining Lactarius traits.

edible_use.notes: Generally treated as edible, though texture/flavor quality varies. App posture should be: edible only with unmistakable blue latex and blue/green-staining Lactarius traits.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

records.scientific_name: North American Lactarius deliciosus-type group, including Lactarius deliciosus var. deterrimus sensu Kuo and Lactarius thyinos
records.notes: Group treatment is safer because the European name Lactarius deliciosus is commonly misapplied in North America, and eastern/northern taxa are still handled inconsistently in guides.
records.field_identification: Latex: orange initially; some taxa bruise green readily; Lactarius thyinos has orange milk and does not bruise green, with red staining around latex over time. Orange to salmon-orange, sometimes zoned; can develop green patches in greening taxa; convex becoming depressed. Orange to pale orange, attached to decurrent; green staining may appear in some taxa after handling. Orange to salmon, sometimes with shallow potholes/scrobicules; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Orange to salmon-orange, sometimes zoned; can develop green patches in greening taxa; convex becoming depressed.
gill_color: Orange to pale orange, attached to decurrent; green staining may appear in some taxa after handling.
stem_color: Orange to salmon, sometimes with shallow potholes/scrobicules; no ring or volva.
mushroom_profile.latex_abundance: variable but usually evident when fresh gills are cut
mushroom_profile.latex_color_change: some taxa bruise green readily; Lactarius thyinos has orange milk and does not bruise green, with red staining around latex over time
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Orange latex is required for this group; white or yellowing latex belongs elsewhere.
staining: Depending on taxon, orange tissue and gills may bruise green; some non-greening taxa slowly stain reddish around latex.
smell: Mild, resinous, fruity, or not distinctive; odor should not be used alone.
taste: Usually mild to slightly bitter; reject hot/acrid specimens.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Lactarius vinaceorufescens / yellowing milkcaps: white latex that quickly turns yellow, not orange latex avoid; this guide should not place yellow-latex milkcaps in the saffron edible bucket Jack-o'-lantern mushroom: orange true-gilled mushroom on wood/buried roots with no latex poisonous GI mushroom; not a milkcap Chanterelles: false ridges rather than true gills and no latex different morphology lane
records.edibility_detail: Edible group after confident orange-latex confirmation, but not species-level in the app unless a specimen is keyed carefully. Do not use the European L. deliciosus as the primary North American label.

edible_use.notes: Edible group after confident orange-latex confirmation, but not species-level in the app unless a specimen is keyed carefully. Do not use the European L. deliciosus as the primary North American label.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

records.notes: Regional relevance is strong: it is specifically described as prolific in northern Michigan. Species-level entry is useful, but the this guide should emphasize latex confirmation versus Russula brevipes.
records.field_identification: Latex: white initially; not a major diagnostic staining species in the app context. Large, white to off-white, later light brownish; roughened/scaly; margin inrolled when young and softly leathery. Close to almost distant; not densely crowded like Lactarius piperatus. Stout, white to off-white; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Large, white to off-white, later light brownish; roughened/scaly; margin inrolled when young and softly leathery.
gill_color: Close to almost distant; not densely crowded like Lactarius piperatus.
stem_color: Stout, white to off-white; no ring or volva.
mushroom_profile.latex_abundance: present when fresh, but may vary with age/dryness
mushroom_profile.latex_color_change: not a major diagnostic staining species in the app context
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Latex separates it from look-alike Russula brevipes.
staining: White cap becomes off-white with light brownish discoloration; surfaces may look roughened, scaly, or dingy with age.
smell: Not strongly distinctive for app use.
taste: Acrid/peppery; do not promote as edible. Taste testing should be framed as an expert ID clue, not casual app advice.
host_trees: often near Russula brevipes in sandy mixed woods
host_filter_tokens: often near Russula brevipes in sandy mixed woods
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Russula brevipes group: very similar at a glance but Russula has no latex when cut knife-cut gills/flesh before any confidence Lactarius piperatus / glaucescens group: much more crowded gills; some members have milk that changes greenish or yellowish acrid white milkcaps are not beginner edibles Destroying angel buttons: Amanita has free gills, ring/veil remnants, and a buried volva/cup at the base; no latex deadly; base inspection mandatory for white gilled mushrooms
records.edibility_detail: Avoid / not recommended. Important as a lobster-mushroom host/look-alike context and as a white milkcap ID training entry.

edible_use.notes: Avoid / not recommended. Important as a lobster-mushroom host/look-alike context and as a white milkcap ID training entry.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

records.scientific_name: Lactarius piperatus / Lactarius glaucescens / Lactarius subvellereus group
records.notes: Group treatment is safer because several white eastern milkcaps overlap heavily, and separating them often hinges on gill crowding, latex color change, cap texture, and taste.
records.field_identification: Latex: white initially; L. piperatus milk stays white and does not dry olive/green; L. glaucescens milk slowly turns olive to pastel green; L. subvellereus milk may yellow slightly. White to off-white; funneling/depressed with age; surface may be smooth, velvety, or roughened depending on member. Often crowded to very crowded in L. piperatus and L. glaucescens; less densely crowded in some look-alikes. White to off-white, brittle, no ring or volva.

cap_color: White to off-white; funneling/depressed with age; surface may be smooth, velvety, or roughened depending on member.
gill_color: Often crowded to very crowded in L. piperatus and L. glaucescens; less densely crowded in some look-alikes.
stem_color: White to off-white, brittle, no ring or volva.
mushroom_profile.latex_color_change: L. piperatus milk stays white and does not dry olive/green; L. glaucescens milk slowly turns olive to pastel green; L. subvellereus milk may yellow slightly
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Latex behavior is one of the main app fields for separating this group.
staining: Some members slowly stain greenish, yellowish, or brown-spotted on gills; timing can take minutes and should be recorded.
smell: Usually not the decisive field trait.
taste: Extremely acrid/peppery. Avoid eating. Do not encourage taste-testing except as expert field note with spit-out warning.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Lactarius deceptivus: larger roughened/scaly cap and gills not as densely crowded also not a beginner edible Russula brevipes group: no latex; brittle Russula flesh/gills can look extremely similar at first glance Destroying angel / white Amanita group: free gills, ring, volva/cup at base, no latex deadly; never identify white gilled mushrooms casually
records.edibility_detail: Avoid / not recommended. Primarily useful as a field-ID warning group for white milkcaps.

edible_use.notes: Avoid / not recommended. Primarily useful as a field-ID warning group for white milkcaps.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

records.notes: Use group treatment for app safety because hairy pink/orange birch milkcaps can be confused, and the practical warning is more important than splitting every close species.
records.field_identification: Latex: white to cream initially; typically does not change color or stain gills in classic L. torminosus. Pinkish to salmon-pink/orange-pink, often zoned; margin inrolled and conspicuously woolly/hairy when young; becomes depressed/funnel-shaped. Pale flesh-colored to pinkish/salmon, narrow, crowded, slightly decurrent. Pale pinkish/flesh-colored, brittle, sometimes hollowing; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Pinkish to salmon-pink/orange-pink, often zoned; margin inrolled and conspicuously woolly/hairy when young; becomes depressed/funnel-shaped.
gill_color: Pale flesh-colored to pinkish/salmon, narrow, crowded, slightly decurrent.
stem_color: Pale pinkish/flesh-colored, brittle, sometimes hollowing; no ring or volva.
mushroom_profile.latex_abundance: usually visible when fresh gills are damaged
mushroom_profile.latex_color_change: typically does not change color or stain gills in classic L. torminosus
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: White latex plus woolly pink cap margin and birch association are the useful app clues.
staining: Cap colors fade from pinkish/orange-pink toward paler or whitish tones with age; latex generally remains white.
smell: Not strongly distinctive; mushroomy or not decisive.
taste: Hot, acrid, strongly peppery; can irritate mouth if over-sampled. Do not encourage taste testing for casual users.
host_trees: mixed woods or edges with birch
host_filter_tokens: mixed woods or edges with birch
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Orange-latex milkcaps: orange latex rather than white latex; often conifer/cedar bog association do not judge by orange-pink cap alone Mild edible milkcaps: lack woolly pink margin and acrid burn woolly/acrid milkcaps should be treated as avoid
records.edibility_detail: Poisonous/avoid in this guide. Some traditions process it heavily, but that is outside this guide’s practical safety lane.

edible_use.notes: Poisonous/avoid in this guide. Some traditions process it heavily, but that is outside this guide’s practical safety lane.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

records.notes: North American usage of L. helvus vs L. aquifluus is taxonomically messy. The this guide should group this as a bog/conifer milkcap with watery latex and strong curry-maple odor.
records.field_identification: Latex: watery, clear, or very pale initially; does not strongly stain gills or flesh. Brownish, orangish brown, pinkish brown, or beige-brown; often depressed/funnel-shaped with age. Pale to tan/cream, attached to decurrent, not the bright orange gills of saffron milkcaps. Usually relatively long compared with cap; brownish/orangish/tan; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Brownish, orangish brown, pinkish brown, or beige-brown; often depressed/funnel-shaped with age.
gill_color: Pale to tan/cream, attached to decurrent, not the bright orange gills of saffron milkcaps.
stem_color: Usually relatively long compared with cap; brownish/orangish/tan; no ring or volva.
mushroom_profile.latex_abundance: usually not copious; can be scant
mushroom_profile.latex_color_change: does not strongly stain gills or flesh
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Watery latex separates it from white-latex weeping milkcaps and orange-latex saffron milkcaps.
staining: Generally little latex staining; odor often intensifies as the mushroom dries.
smell: Strong curry, fenugreek, burned maple syrup, or maple-sugar odor, especially when dried.
taste: Do not recommend tasting; avoid as food.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Lactifluus volemus group: white copious latex that may brown-stain and often fishy odor; not watery latex in sphagnum bog do not confuse food-scent/milk behavior Candy-cap / maple-scented milkcaps: small brown milkcaps with maple odor can overlap by smell; habitat, latex, size, and cap/gill details matter avoid eating scent-only IDs
records.edibility_detail: Avoid / poisonous-risk. Strong smell is not a green light; this guide should warn against treating maple/curry odor as an edible confirmation.

edible_use.notes: Avoid / poisonous-risk. Strong smell is not a green light; this guide should warn against treating maple/curry odor as an edible confirmation.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

records.scientific_name: Lactarius vinaceorufescens / Lactarius chrysorrheus-type yellowing milkcaps
records.notes: Practical field rule is stronger than species splitting: white latex that rapidly or slowly yellows is not the same as orange-latex edible saffron milkcaps.
records.field_identification: Latex: white initially; turns yellow after exposure to air, sometimes within seconds depending on taxon. Cinnamon-pink, redder, pale, or zoned depending on species; can resemble edible milkcaps from above. Pale pinkish, cream, or cinnamon-tinged; may spot reddish/cinnamon with age or handling. Usually smooth in L. vinaceorufescens; some related species have potholes/scrobicules.

cap_color: Cinnamon-pink, redder, pale, or zoned depending on species; can resemble edible milkcaps from above.
gill_color: Pale pinkish, cream, or cinnamon-tinged; may spot reddish/cinnamon with age or handling.
stem_color: Usually smooth in L. vinaceorufescens; some related species have potholes/scrobicules.
mushroom_profile.latex_color_change: turns yellow after exposure to air, sometimes within seconds depending on taxon
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: The this guide should make yellowing latex a hard separation trait.
staining: Latex and cut surfaces may yellow quickly; gills may develop pinkish, cinnamon, reddish, or brownish spotting depending on species.
smell: Not the main field character; may be mild to sharp/fragrant depending on species.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Orange-latex milkcaps / saffron group: true orange latex rather than white latex turning yellow do not put yellowing white-latex milkcaps in edible saffron group White peppery milkcaps: white cap and crowded gills; latex may remain white, green, or yellowish by species acrid milkcaps are not beginner edibles
records.edibility_detail: Avoid / not recommended. Use as a warning group attached to orange-latex milkcap searches.

edible_use.notes: Avoid / not recommended. Use as a warning group attached to orange-latex milkcap searches.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

records.scientific_name: Russula parvovirescens and related North American green-cracking Russulas formerly treated under Russula virescens concepts
records.notes: Edible green-cracking Russulas are practical, but the old R. virescens concept is taxonomically messy in North America. Group treatment avoids importing European certainty.
records.field_identification: Russula-style entry: brittle/chalky flesh and no latex when cut. Green, blue-green, or gray-green with cracked/quilted patches; often drier and patterned rather than smooth slimy green. White to cream; brittle; attached; no latex. White, brittle/chalky Russula stem; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Green, blue-green, or gray-green with cracked/quilted patches; often drier and patterned rather than smooth slimy green.
gill_color: White to cream; brittle; attached; no latex.
stem_color: White, brittle/chalky Russula stem; no ring or volva.
staining: Generally not a dramatic staining mushroom; cap may crack/quilt and fade with age.
taste: Only mild-tasting green-cracking Russulas belong here. Avoid acrid/hot Russulas.
host_trees: often oak/hardwood settings depending on member
host_filter_tokens: often oak/hardwood settings depending on member
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Amanita phalloides-like green Amanitas: Amanitas have ring/volva/free gills and fibrous flesh, not brittle Russula flesh deadly Amanita risk means base/ring/gill attachment must be checked Other green Russulas: may be smooth-capped rather than quilted/cracked; some require spore print/taste/chemistry/microscopy group entry should not overpromise species ID Acrid Russulas: hot/peppery taste, different cap patterns/colors avoid acrid Russulas
records.edibility_detail: Good edible group for advanced foragers when mild and confidently Russula, but keep app posture conservative. Require no ring, no volva, brittle flesh, no latex, and quilted/cracked green cap.

edible_use.notes: Good edible group for advanced foragers when mild and confidently Russula, but keep app posture conservative. Require no ring, no volva, brittle flesh, no latex, and quilted/cracked green cap.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

records.scientific_name: Russula emetica group and similar acrid red Russulas
records.notes: The practical app hazard is red Russulas with acrid/hot taste causing GI illness. Species-level precision is less useful than flagging the avoid group.
records.field_identification: Russula-style entry: brittle/chalky flesh and no latex when cut. Red to deep red, often darker centrally; smooth cap surface; cap skin may peel. White to pale cream, brittle Russula gills; no latex. White or pale, brittle/chalky; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Red to deep red, often darker centrally; smooth cap surface; cap skin may peel.
gill_color: White to pale cream, brittle Russula gills; no latex.
stem_color: White or pale, brittle/chalky; no ring or volva.
staining: Often not a strong bruising/staining entry; cap may fade or vary from bright to darker red.
smell: Usually not as useful as taste and habitat; some related red Russulas may have distinctive odors but this guide should not rely on smell alone.
taste: Hot, peppery, acrid, or burning taste is the warning trait. Do not recommend casual taste testing; if expert uses taste, spit out completely.
host_trees: spring through fall in Michigan per MSU source
host_filter_tokens: spring through fall in Michigan per MSU source
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Shrimp Russula group: mild taste and fishy/shrimp odor with age; not hot/acrid do not eat red Russulas based on color Other mild red Russulas: mild taste, different odor/spore print/ecology; species ID often hard group-level caution is safer Amanita muscaria buttons or red/orange Amanitas: Amanitas have universal veil remnants, ring/volva/basal bulb features; Russulas are brittle and lack veil structures separate dangerous genus check
records.edibility_detail: Poisonous/avoid. Severe gastrointestinal upset is the app message; not deadly in typical North American treatment but absolutely not a food entry.

edible_use.notes: Poisonous/avoid. Severe gastrointestinal upset is the app message; not deadly in typical North American treatment but absolutely not a food entry.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
